claim: The verifier agent is a two-agent multi-agent pattern: one builder agent (Opus 4.7) codes, one verifier agent (GPT 5.5) validates claims unprompted; this breaks through the review bottleneck in agent coding
actionable: For your next agent project, add a second verifier agent that validates every builder output; connect them via Unix socket so the verifier kicks off automatically after the builder completes
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Spend tokens to save time: the verifier agent costs 5x more tokens but saves you time by catching issues the builder missed, validating architectural decisions, and providing structured feedback
actionable: Calculate your hourly rate; if a verifier costs 5 API dollars to save 10 minutes of your review time, it's worth the cost. Budget for verification as part of your token spend
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Break prompts into atomic claims (small, provable assertions); verifiers validate each claim independently, making verification deterministic and testable
actionable: When prompting agents, decompose requests into atomic claims ('find all SQLite DBs', 'extract table schemas', 'validate relationships'); teach verifiers to check each claim separately
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Verifiers should report what they could NOT verify; this feedback loop lets you improve your system prompt incrementally based on what the verifier couldn't validate
actionable: Design verifier reports with a 'what could you not verify' section; periodically review these gaps and add them as constraints to your verifier's system prompt
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Ownership of your agent harness (Pi agent over cloud-hosted agents) lets you implement custom verification rules, bash policies, and tool restrictions that you can't do in generic platforms
actionable: For critical agent systems, use Pi agent or build your own harness; restrict bash tool to only run approved scripts, implement custom tool policies, and control verification rules
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: The bash tool is the most dangerous tool you can give agents; restrict it to only execute approved scripts, never allow unrestricted bash execution in production agents
actionable: In your agent harness, add a bash policy that only allows specific whitelisted scripts (e.g., only /scripts/verify.sh); block any other bash calls immediately
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Multi-agent orchestration (coordinated agents working together) is more powerful than single-model benchmarks suggest; GPT 5.5 AND Opus 4.7 working together beats either alone
actionable: Stop comparing models in isolation; instead, design multi-agent systems that pair complementary models (e.g., GPT-4 for reasoning, Claude for coding) to beat either alone
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Model benchmarks are missing the multi-agent dimension; they test single models in isolation but don't show what happens when models coordinate as teams with specialized roles
actionable: When evaluating models for your system, don't rely on individual benchmarks; test how models work together in your specific multi-agent workflow
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Template your engineering (codify rules in agent system prompts) instead of ad-hoc prompting; force yourself out of the 'vibe coding' loop by building rules into your verifier agent
actionable: Build custom agent harnesses with locked system prompts that enforce your rules; if something breaks, improve the prompt template rather than issuing one-off fix prompts
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: The core constraint in agentic coding is the review bottleneck (not planning or execution); verifier agents directly address this by automating careful review with verification rules
actionable: Identify your biggest agentic bottleneck (usually review); build a verifier agent with specific rules to address it; measure time saved vs token cost to validate ROI
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU

claim: Agentic engineering is becoming the key job; engineers who own their agent harnesses and orchestrate teams of agents will outperform those stuck doing single-agent 'vibe coding'
actionable: Invest in learning multi-agent systems, custom harness development, and verification patterns; these skills compound and are becoming table stakes for top engineers
source_title: GPT-5.5 VERIFIED Opus 4.7: A Pi Coding Agent That REVIEWS Like YOU
